What Does Vandermissen Brickworks Site Represent?
October 1871, Peshtigo fire destroyed many Belgian farms small towns along west side Door Peninsula.
Belgians rebuilt after fire using bricks instead logs construction.
household brickmaking industry developed produce bricks.
Many these distinctive brick structures survive today give region architectural character.
Vandermissen Brickworks operated early 1899 ceased operation between 1905 1917.
Other small brickworks operation during this time include Macco Brickworks south Kewaunee County Peters Brickworks located northeast Vandermissen.
Vandermissen Brickworks first late 19th early twentieth century brickworks excavated region.
Archaeology site recovered evidence steps typically associated with process hand-making bricks.
Local brickworks would have been very common during rebuilding period following Peshtigo Fire.
However, very documented
Bricks were made five-step process.
Clay hand from local deposits fall left during winter weather "soften".
Once clay been softened, ground screened.
Then clay into "ring pit" "pug mill" sand water were added produce brick clay mix.
Bricks Vandermissen site were hand-pressed into wooden forms lined with sand prevent sticking.
Bricks were dreid remove moisture then stacked into piles called "hacks".
Bricks were fired kiln known "brick clamp", made bricks, constructed with gaps allow flow air.
fire ignited clamp firing process typically took several days.
